title: Soliloquy Of The Spanish Cloister
content:
I.

آ آ آ آ GR-R-R there go, my heartï؟½s abhorrence!
آ آ آ آ Water your damned flower-pots, do!
آ آ آ آ If hate killed men, Brother Lawrence,
آ آ آ آ Godï؟½s blood, would not mine kill you!
آ آ آ آ What? your myrtle-bush wants trimming?
آ آ آ آ Oh, that rose has prior claims
آ آ آ آ Needs its leaden vase filled brimming?
آ آ آ آ Hell dry you up with its flames!

II.

آ آ آ آ At the meal we sit together:
آ آ آ آ Salve tibi! I must hear
آ آ آ آ Wise talk of the kind of weather,
آ آ آ آ Sort of season, time of year:
آ آ آ آ Not a plenteous cork-crop: scarcely
آ آ آ آ Dare we hope oak-galls, I doubt:
آ آ آ آ Whatï؟½s the Latin name for ï؟½parsleyï؟½?
آ آ آ آ Whatï؟½s the Greek name for Swineï؟½s Snout?

III.

آ آ آ آ Whew! Weï؟½ll have our platter burnished,
آ آ آ آ Laid with care on our own shelf!
آ آ آ آ With a fire-new spoon weï؟½re furnished,
آ آ آ آ And a goblet for ourself,
آ آ آ آ Rinsed like something sacrificial
آ آ آ آ Ere ï؟½tis fit to touch our chaps
آ آ آ آ Marked with L. for our initial!
آ آ آ آ (He-he! There his lily snaps!)

IV.

آ آ آ آ Saint, forsooth! While brown Dolores
آ آ آ آ Squats outside the Convent bank
آ آ آ آ With Sanchicha, telling stories,
آ آ آ آ Steeping tresses in the tank,
آ آ آ آ Blue-black, lustrous, thick like horsehairs,
آ آ آ آ Canï؟½t I see his dead eye glow,
آ آ آ آ Bright as ï؟½twere a Barbary corsairï؟½s?
آ آ آ آ (That is, if heï؟½d let it show!)

V.

آ آ آ آ When he finishes refection,
آ آ آ آ Knife and fork he never lays
آ آ آ آ Cross-wise, to my recollection,
آ آ آ آ As do I, in Jesuï؟½s praise.
آ آ آ آ I the Trinity illustrate,
آ آ آ آ Drinking watered orange-pulp
آ آ آ آ In three sips the Arian frustrate;
آ آ آ آ fWhile he drains his at one gulp.

VI.

آ آ آ آ Oh, those melons? If heï؟½s able
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½re to have a feast! so nice!
آ آ آ آ One goes to the Abbotï؟½s table,
آ آ آ آ All of us get each a slice.
آ آ آ آ How go on your flowers? None double
آ آ آ آ Not one fruit-sort can you spy?
آ آ آ آ Strange! And I, too, at such trouble,
آ آ آ آ Keep them close-nipped on the sly!

VII.

آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a great text in Galatians,
آ آ آ آ Once you trip on it, entails
آ آ آ آ Twenty-nine distinct damnations,
آ آ آ آ One sure, if another fails:
آ آ آ آ If I trip him just a-dying,
آ آ آ آ Sure of heaven as sure can be,
آ آ آ آ Spin him round and send him flying
آ آ آ آ Off to Hell, a Manichee?

VIII.

آ آ آ آ Or, my scrofulous French novel
آ آ آ آ On grey paper with blunt type!
آ آ آ آ Simply glance at it, you grovel
آ آ آ آ Hand and foot in Belialï؟½s gripe:
آ آ آ آ If I double down its pages
آ آ آ آ At the woeful sixteenth print,
آ آ آ آ When he gathers his greengages,
آ آ آ آ Ope a sieve and slip it inï؟½t?

IX.

آ آ آ آ Or, thereï؟½s Satan! one might venture
آ آ آ آ Pledge oneï؟½s soul to him, yet leave
آ آ آ آ Such a flaw in the indenture
آ آ آ آ As heï؟½d miss till, past retrieve,
آ آ آ آ Blasted lay that rose-acacia
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½re so proud of! Hy, Zy, Hine . . .
آ آ آ آ ï؟½St, thereï؟½s Vespers! Plena gratiï؟½
آ آ آ آ Ave, Virgo! Gr-r-r you swine!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning, titled "Soliloquy Of The Spanish Cloister", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
